title: Nagaoka supermetal in the particle-doped triangular Hubbard model
authors: Rui Cao, Xiangyue Zhang, Hui Tan, Jian-Shu Xu, Yuan-Yao He, Jianmin Yuan, Yongqiang Li
abstract: While the interplay of correlations and geometric frustration in doped Mott insulators provides a fertile ground for exotic quantum phases, the nature of the metallic state emerging upon particle doping remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the triangular-lattice Hubbard model with particle doping and provide compelling evidence for an intrinsic, interaction-driven quantum state, which we term the Nagaoka supermetal. This state is characterized by a sublinear temperature dependence in the DC resistivity, along with singular behaviors in the charge compressibility and zero-frequency spectral weight. To understand the origin of these singular properties, we derive an effective low-energy model and demonstrate that a higher-order Van Hove singularity emerges from the reconstructed dispersion. This singularity gives rise to a power-law divergence in the density of states, capturing the anomalous properties observed in the supermetallic regime. Our findings offer a new perspective on non-Fermi liquid states in geometrically frustrated systems and are directly accessible in current ultracold atom experiments.
summary: The paper identifies a new quantum state called the Nagaoka supermetal in the particle-doped triangular Hubbard model. This state arises from an interaction-induced higher-order Van Hove singularity that causes anomalous transport properties like sublinear resistivity. The findings provide a new perspective on non-Fermi liquid behavior in geometrically frustrated systems.
main_problem: Understanding the nature of the metallic state emerging upon particle doping in a frustrated Mott insulator and identifying the origin of its non-Fermi liquid behavior.
main_result: Discovery of the 'Nagaoka supermetal' state, characterized by sublinear temperature-dependent resistivity and power-law divergences in charge compressibility, driven by an interaction-induced higher-order Van Hove singularity.
method: Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) using a continuous-time hybridization expansion solver, supplemented by second-order perturbation theory and effective low-energy Hamiltonian derivation.
model_or_system: The two-dimensional triangular-lattice Hubbard model in the strongly correlated regime (U/t = 12), specifically focusing on the particle-doped regime.
key_observables: DC resistivity, charge compressibility, zero-frequency spectral weight, density of states, and nearest-neighbor spin correlation functions.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (U/t = 12), doping level (delta), temperature (T/t), and kinetic renormalization factor (Z).
main_assumptions: Neglect of vertex corrections in resistivity calculations and the use of the bubble approximation for transport properties; cluster size is limited to Nc <= 6 due to the fermion sign problem.
figures_summary: A phase map showing the transition from Mott insulator to various regimes (FL, SM, PG, NS) and a finite-temperature phase diagram demonstrating the robustness of the NS state.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of doped Mott insulators, describes the Hubbard model and DCA methodology, presents the discovery of the Nagaoka supermetal and its transport signatures, derives the effective Hamiltonian and the higher-order Van Hove singularity mechanism, and concludes with implications for experimental observations.
why_interesting: The discovery of an intrinsic, interaction-driven non-Fermi liquid state with power-law divergences is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and is directly accessible in ultracold atom experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Emergence of a Nagaoka supermetal (NS) state in the particle-doped triangular lattice. (a) Schematic of low- temperature phases mapped by distinct transport, thermo- dynamic, and spectroscopic signatures. Under hole doping, the system exhibits a crossover from a Mott insulator to a Fermi liquid (FL) through intermediate pseudogap (PG) and strange metal (SM) regimes. In contrast, on the particle- doped side, the SM behavior gives way to a NS state that emerges alongside Nagaoka ferromagnetic correlations at high doping. Bottom panel: Schematic of the corresponding mag- netic order. (b) Finite-temperature phase diagram. The NS state exhibits remarkable robustness, persisting up to T/t...

FIG. 2. Signatures of the Nagaoka-driven NS state. Temperature dependence of (a) DC resistivity ρ, (b) NN spin correlation ⟨Sz i Sz j ⟩c, and (c) charge compressibility χc for various doping δ. The NS state is characterized by sublinear ρ ∼T α with α < 1 [dashed lines in (a)] and power-law divergent χc ∼T −γ with γ > 0 [dashed lines in (c)]. These features emerge as Nagaoka ferromagnetic correlations develop (b), signaling a distinct departure from the high-temperature regime characterized by ρ = AT + B [dotted line in (a)] and antiferromagnetic correlations. Pronounced transport and thermodynamic anomalies manifest within the regime of well-developed ferromagnetic correlations, consistent...

FIG. 3. Characteristics of the Nagaoka-driven HOVHS in the particle-doped regime. (a) Kinetic renormalization fac- tor Z as a function of doping and temperature. The dashed line denotes the Z = 0.75 contour, marking the onset of the interaction-induced HOVHS and coinciding with the emer- gence of sublinear resistivity [Fig. 2(a)]. (b) Zero-frequency spectral weight A(ω = 0) as a function of doping and temper- ature. The dashed lines represent the fits to their correspond- ing low-temperature data sets. The interaction strength is set to U/t = 12.

FIG. 4. Impact of interaction strength U on transport and magnetic properties. (a) DC resistivity ρ as a function of tem- perature T/t for different interaction strengths U/t. The inset shows the onset temperature Tc of the crossover into the NS regime, extracted from ρ. (b) Temperature dependence of the NN spin correlation function. The sign-change temperature shifts toward lower T as U/t decreases. The doping concen- tration is fixed at δ = 0.5.
